title: Writing
text:
Essays
  Writing
  
    On measurement, evidence, and the gap between how decisions
    are meant to be made and how they are made in practice.
  






  
    
      Data Storytelling
      8 min read
    
    What Hip-Hop Taught Me About Construct Validity
    When you try to measure something as contested as
    lyricism, every methodological flaw in organizational metrics shows up
    in sharp relief.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Criminal Justice · Measurement
      10 min read
    
    Counted Wrong: Data, Young Adults, and the
    Criminal Justice System
    The categories we use to count people in the
    legal system were not designed with young adults in mind — and that
    design failure has consequences.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Metrics · Policy
      7 min read
    
    When the Metric Becomes the Policy
    How measurement decisions quietly become legal
    and organizational ones — and why that matters for anyone who builds
    or uses performance measures.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Aging & the Law
      9 min read
    
    The Mercy We Wrote Down and Never Used
    Compassionate release exists in almost every
    state's statutes and almost nowhere in practice. What a law that's
    present but unused tells us about the difference between writing a
    policy and measuring whether it works.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Aging · Measurement
      6 min read
    
    How Old Is Old? The Age the Law Pretends to Know
    The legal system fixes hard numbers — 18, 50, 65 —
    onto a boundary that no developmental science draws. A short piece on
    operationalizing a category that has no natural boundary.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Prediction · Risk
      8 min read
    
    Trust the Checklist: Why Expert Judgment Keeps Losing
    Decades of evidence say a two-variable rule
    outpredicts the seasoned professional — in pretrial risk, in clinical
    forecasting, almost everywhere we've checked. Why intuition retains its
    hold regardless, and what that preference costs.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Data Storytelling · Courts
      7 min read
    
    Cultivating a Court's Data Story
    Most court data is buried in reports that go
    unread. A field guide for turning administrative numbers into something a
    judge, a funder, or a community will read and remember.
    Coming soon
  




More essays in progress.

title: Selected Work

Publications

    any point for the citation; curated highlights follow.

title: Selected Work
text:
Research areas


  Aging & the Law
  
    How the legal system meets older adults — as litigants, victims, caregivers,
    and prisoners.
  
  
    
      
    
    
      Book · APA, 2025
      Aging and the Criminal Legal System
      
        Community Services, Courts, Corrections, and Policy.
        With E.M. Brank. Division 41 American Psychology-Law book series,
        American Psychological Association. Brings behavioral science research
        to bear on the legal and psychological challenges facing older adults
        across the criminal legal system.
      
      
        View at APA →
      
    
  
  
    
      Brank, E.M., & Wylie, L.E. (2021).
      Legal responsibility among the young and the elderly.
      Annual Review of Law and Social Science.
    
    
      Wylie, L.E., Knutson, A., & Greene, E. (2018).
      A review of federal and state compassionate release laws:
      Presence, utilization, and implications.
      Psychology, Public Policy, and Law.
    
  

  Juvenile & Emerging Adult Justice
  
    Diversion, risk and needs, and the program features that change outcomes
    for young people in the system.
  
  
    
      Kotlaja, M., & Wylie, L.E. (2023).
      Electronically monitored youth: Stigma and negative
      social functioning.
      Crime & Delinquency.
    
    
      Deal, T., Cienfuegos, A., & Wylie, L.E. (2022).
      Meeting the needs of emerging adults in the justice system.
      Trends in State Courts.
    
    
      Wylie, L.E., Clinkinbeard, S., & Hobbs, A. (2019).
      The application of risk-needs-responsivity programming
      in juvenile diversion. Criminal Justice and Behavior.
    
    
      Wylie, L.E., & Rufino, K.A. (2018).
      The impact of victimization and mental health on
      recidivism for early system-involved juvenile offenders.
      Law and Human Behavior.
    
  

  Courts, Data & Communication
  
    Helping courts measure, understand, and communicate the story of their
    own work.
  
  
    
      Wylie, L.E. (2025).
      Cultivating a court's data STORY: A guide for court
      leaders to develop storytelling skills and effective communications.
      Trends in State Courts.
    
    
      Wylie, L.E., Hoetger, L.A., Hazen, K., Haby, J., &
      Brank, E.M. (2018).
      Forty years of Law and Human Behavior: A
      content analysis. Scientometrics.
    
  

  Evidence & Empirical Legal Studies
  
    Empirical answers to doctrinal questions — from arbitration outcomes to
    voice identification evidence.
  
  
    
      Franck, S., & Wylie, L.E. (2015).
      Predicting outcomes in investment treaty arbitration.
      Duke Law Journal.

title: Data with a Plot
text:
Lindsey E. Wylie, J.D., Ph.D.
      
        Rigorous analytic thinking, made accessible — through unexpected
        datasets, practical methodology, and visualizations built to be
        understood and remembered.

title: Data with a Plot
text:
What I Do

  
  
    I study how data informs decision-making and legal policies--with specific
    attention to how measurement influences outcomes

    
      Ph.D., Social Psychology · concentration in Research 
      Design & Data Analysis
      J.D. · concentration in Health Law
    

    I am especially interested in the unintended consequences of legal
    decisions and interventions, which are often found at the intersection of 
    well-intentioned law or policy, and the reality of complex systems. My interests 
    are shaped by my experience as a system-involved young person and my desire to 
    highlight under-studied research topics.

    Before joining the National Center for State Courts, I directed
    research at the University of Nebraska Omaha's Juvenile Justice
    Institute. I have led federally funded research projects and multi-state
    evaluations across topics in pretrial and diversion, behavioral health in courts, 
    and family violence.

    See selected work & CV →

title: Data with a Plot
text:
How I Do It


  
    
      
        
        
        
        
      
    
    
      Applied Research Methods and Design
      
        Randomized studies and quasi-experimental methods across law, psychology,
        and public policy — designs matched to the question and to the
        constraints of real-world settings.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    
      Tailored Program Evaluation and Recommendations
      
        Process and outcome evaluations of courts, diversion, and pretrial
        programs — combining quantitative and qualitative methods to produce
        recommendations programs can act on.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        
          
          
          
        
        
        
        
      
    
    
      Tests of Measurement Validity and Reliability
      
        Building and validating screening tools and latent-trait measures —
        evidence that an instrument measures what it claims to, consistently.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
      
    
    
      Practical Storytelling with Data
      
        Professional and graduate teaching that turns rigorous analysis into work a
        judge, funder, or community will read and remember — taught on datasets
        whose flaws are part of the lesson.

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text: This page documents every variable in the Hip-Hop Periodic Table dataset, the constructs behind the five lyricism scores, how scores were assigned, and the sampling frame the dataset draws from. The same content ships as the Data Dictionary sheet inside the Excel workbook, which is the single source of truth.

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text:
The five constructs
Each act is scored 1–10 on five dimensions of lyricism. Scores are a synthesis of published criticism and hip-hop scholarship — no NLP analysis was run. Each dimension has a named anchor at 10 so the top of the scale means the same thing across scorers.








Construct
What it measures
Anchor at 10




Rhyme Density
Frequency and complexity of rhyme: internal rhyme, multisyllabic chains, rhymes per bar, scheme inventiveness
Rakim — pioneered internal and multisyllabic rhyme as standard practice


Vocab Breadth
Lexical diversity: range of unique words and registers across the catalog
Aesop Rock — per published NLP studies of unique-word counts in rap


Storytelling
Narrative construction: character, plot, perspective shifts, arc within songs and across albums
Scarface


Metaphor/Imagery
Density and originality of figurative language: extended metaphor, concrete imagery, double meanings
—


Conceptual Depth
Thematic ambition and coherence: album-level concepts, social and philosophical substance
Kendrick Lamar — 10 is reserved for album-length conceptual execution at the highest tier



The Composite Score is the unweighted mean of the five dimensions, rounded to one decimal. Equal weighting is itself a methods decision — one of the module’s core discussion prompts.

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text:
Scoring method

Basis: synthesis of published music criticism, academic hip-hop scholarship (Bradley, Chang, Kajikawa, Rabaka), and documented critical consensus. Scores are judgments, not computations.
Scale: integers 1–10 per dimension; anchored at the top of each scale (table above).
Confidence flag: every act carries an H/M/L rating of how much to trust its scores, based on the depth of its critical record (rubric below).

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text:
Confidence rubric








Flag
Meaning
Criteria




H — High
Extensive critical record
Sustained critical/academic attention; multiple long-form analyses of the act’s lyrical craft; consistent reception across sources; a body of work large enough that scores are stable across albums


M — Medium
Moderate documentation
Meets 1–2 of the High criteria: historically significant but under-analyzed, recently emergent with an incomplete body of work, contested reception, or regional/underground significance without mainstream scholarly attention. Treat as provisional


L — Low
Thin or contested record
Limited formal analysis, very recent emergence, single-source scores, or reception clouded by non-lyrical controversy. Treat as initial estimates requiring verification



Recommended verification for M/L acts: RateYourMusic critical consensus, Pitchfork / The Wire discography reviews, Google Scholar (artist + “lyricism”), and genre publications (The Source, XXL retrospectives, Passion of the Weiss).

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text:
Sampling frame & scope rules






Two scope rules merit particular attention:

Scene = scene, not birthplace. Acts are assigned to the US scene they are professionally rooted in — label home and scene affiliation during their defining run (Da Brat is South via So So Def, though born in Chicago). Region rolls scenes up to the four coast-level categories the culture itself uses: East Coast (NYC + Philly), West Coast (LA + Bay Area), South, and Midwest.
Era = defining run, not debut year. Debut years overlap era boundaries by design; the era reflects when the act’s defining work landed.

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text: Variable definitions

title: Data Dictionary & Codebook
text:
Known biases
The dataset over-represents critically acclaimed acts (vs. commercially dominant ones), male acts, Golden Age NYC, and English-language rap. International and non-Anglophone rap is excluded from the frame entirely — a documented scope decision. The dashboard section “Who Is In the Data?” quantifies these gaps and turns them into teaching prompts on sampling bias.

title: Projects
text:
Work
  Projects
  
    Applied research, teaching tools, and data storytelling experiments.
    Each project is built around a real methodological question.
  




  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    Hip-Hop Periodic Table
    
      A fully documented dataset of 173 hip-hop acts — 130 solo artists and 43
      groups — scored across five lyricism dimensions, with per-artist confidence
      ratings and a published data dictionary. Built to teach measurement validity,
      sampling bias, composite scoring, and uncertainty communication — using a
      subject that provokes genuine debate.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Excel / readxl
      Interactive
    
    
    
      Explore the periodic table →
    
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    The Barnum Test
    
      Does your personality type measure anything at all? A 90-second interactive
      exercise builds a personalized profile, asks how accurate it feels, then
      reveals that every participant receives the identical profile — and that the
      answers were never used. A clear, memorable lesson in construct validity.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Construct validity
      Interactive
    
    
    
      Take the Barnum Test →
    
  

  
    Applied Research · Criminal Justice
    Counted Wrong: Emerging Adults & the Crossroads Study
    
      A close analysis of longitudinal data from the Crossroads Study — following
      young people through their first contact with the justice system — to illustrate
      the complexity of where adolescence ends and adulthood begins. The legal system
      draws a bright line at age 18, but human development does not. A project about
      what happens when a measured boundary is treated as a biological one.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      Longitudinal data
      Developmental measurement
      Law & policy
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Applied Research · Aging & the Law
    A Right That Exists on Paper: Compassionate Release
    
      Nearly every state and the federal system has a compassionate or medical
      release law on the books — a legal mechanism to release people who are dying,
      gravely ill, or simply too old to pose a risk. Almost none are used.
      Drawing on a fifty-state review and the data behind Aging and the Criminal
      Legal System (APA, 2025), this project measures the distance between a
      law's presence and its utilization — and asks what an unused
      statute reveals about how a system measures mercy.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      50-state legal review
      Aging & corrections
      Law & policy
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    How Old Is Old?
    
      A 60-second exercise: set a slider to the age at which someone becomes
      "old," "a senior," "too old to drive," or "too old to start over." The
      responses reveal how little consensus exists — and how the law fixes hard
      numbers (18, 50, 65) onto a boundary with no basis in nature. A study in
      operationalization, and a companion to Counted Wrong at the other
      end of the lifespan.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Operationalization
      Interactive
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    Trust Your Gut?
    
      Review a set of realistic case files and predict who returns to court and
      who does not. Your accuracy is then scored against a simple two-variable
      statistical rule that consistently outperforms the reader — a pointed
      demonstration in applied prediction that expert intuition routinely loses
      to a checklist.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Actuarial vs. clinical
      Interactive
    
    
    Coming soon
  




More projects in progress.

title: Teaching
text:
Education
  
  
    Data storytelling and applied research methods for professionals.
  
  
    Recently presented Telling Your Court's Data Story at the
    National Association for Court Management (2025).

title: Teaching
text:
How I teach
I teach with real datasets, flaws and all. Real data has gaps, debatable scores, and choices somebody made but never wrote down. That is what people face at work, so that is what we practice on.
The six ideas I come back to most often:

Construct validity Sampling and bias Multidimensional measurement Uncertainty communication Visualization design Narrative framing

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching materials

The Hip-Hop Periodic Table: Data Storytelling Module
A complete 90-minute session built around a dataset of 147 hip-hop acts, each scored on five parts of lyricism. Designed for professional and graduate audiences.
Covers: all six ideas above.
The materials:

Interactive periodic table: the branded, filterable artist table
Analytics dashboard: a companion Quarto dashboard with charts
Data dictionary: what each score means, how scores were assigned, how much to trust them, and known biases
Excel workbook: the full dataset (Artist Database, Confidence Guide, and Data Dictionary sheets)







TipSession design note



The session opens with a quick exercise: rate a favorite artist’s lyricism on a scale of 1 to 10, right away, no second-guessing. Then comes the real question. What did you just measure? Working that out takes a good 45 minutes, and by the end people are debating construct validity using their own performance measures as examples.

title: Teaching
section: Topics I teach and write about
text:
Topics I teach and write about


Measurement and metrics

Building performance measures that hold up
When composite scores help and when they mislead
Communicating confidence and uncertainty to non-technical stakeholders
The politics of how organizational metrics get designed


Data visualization

Choosing chart types that match your argument
Designing for uncertainty, not false precision
Interactive vs. static, and when to use each
Common visualization mistakes in stakeholder reporting
